title: Senior Director of Marketing & Ecommerce
dossier_summary: Paul Fredrick: men's premium dress shirts, founded 1986 by Paul Sacher selling direct via 2x2 WSJ ads + mail-order catalog. ClearLight Partners (PE) acquired 2018. New CEO Erica Lee from FullBeauty Brands. Just opened first brick-and-mortar in Georgetown M Street. Active monthly catalog. Hiring CRM Analyst late 2025.
email1_body: karly, opening the M Street store while still running a monthly catalog is a rare setup. most brands pick a lane. the interesting question with a new physical door is what it does to the mail-acquired segment of the file, since those buyers tend to behave very differently than digitally-acquired ones once you give them a store to walk into. has the team started pulling that cohort apart yet, or is it still one blended customer file?
email2_body: one more thought. brands we work with at belardi wong (bombas, serena & lily, vera bradley, talbots, lands' end) consistently see 103% higher ltv on mail-acquired vs digitally-acquired buyers. with paul fredrick's catalog dna going back to the wsj 2x2 days, the file is probably already showing that gap. worth knowing the number before the next drop is planned.
email3_body: karly, with erica lee coming over from fullbeauty, the catalog cadence conversation probably looks different now than it did 18 months ago. she has seen what drops per year does to a file at scale. most premium brands we work with find their productivity lift in year one comes from format and frequency testing, not new creative. open to comparing notes on what cadence paul fredrick is running today?
email4_subject: re: georgetown store + the catalog file
email4_body: karly, last note from me. 25 years running direct mail for premium dtc and retail, 300+ brands, about a billion pieces a year. on existing catalog programs we typically see a 20-30% productivity lift in year one from segmentation and format testing alone. if it is useful, happy to send a short read on how the bombas and talbots files are structured. worth a look?

title: Sr. Director of Ecommerce and Digital Marketing
dossier_summary: Founder-led, never took outside investors. ~$100M brand targeting $200M by 2027. 42% wholesale / 58% DTC. 3 new stores/year planned. Launching menswear + accessories. Recent openings: Palm Beach, UK + existing in Tribeca, Madison Ave, East Hampton, Greenwich, LA.
email1_body: sam, the $200M by 2027 math is interesting given only 3 new stores a year and 42% still in wholesale. that puts a lot of weight on the DTC customer file and on the menswear launch pulling existing buyers into a second category. the premium tier brands we run mail for (STAUD, Reformation, Madein, Paige) see mail-acquired customers come in with 103% higher LTV than digitally-acquired. happy to share what the file segmentation looks like at your AOV band if useful.
email2_body: quick follow up sam. for a founder-led premium brand at your AOV, the segmentation question we usually start with is: what does the top decile of the file actually look like by frequency, and are they being mailed differently than the rest. that one cut tends to surface 20-30% productivity in year one without changing acquisition spend.
email3_body: menswear into an existing premium womenswear file is one of the cleanest brand-led mail use cases we see. the women in the file already trust the aesthetic, and a physical drop into the household reaches the partner too. STAUD and Madein both used mail this way for category extensions. 3-8x ROAS on new customers in those tests. worth a 20 min walk-through?
email4_body: sam, last note from me on this thread. if direct mail isn't on the roadmap for the $200M push, totally understand. if it ever is, the move at your tier is usually 2-4 brand-led drops per year into the top frequency segments of the customer file, not a catalog program. happy to leave you with the STAUD and Reformation case studies for whenever the timing fits.

title: Senior Director of Marketing
dossier_summary: Acquired by New Era in Aug 2024 (combined ~$2B revenue, brands stay separate). Wholesale ~80%. Owns Red Sox Team Store at 19 Jersey Street (largest of all 30 MLB clubs) + Newbury St flagship + Northshore Mall. Licensed MLB/NFL/NHL/NBA/NASCAR + 900+ colleges. 'Father of All Dad Hats' campaign positioning.
email1_subject: fenway team store + the rest of the file
email1_body: kelsey, the jersey street store being the biggest team store in MLB is a fun stat, but the more interesting one is what it means for your customer file. that door alone is generating a postseason buyer profile that looks nothing like a wholesale-acquired customer. since the new era close, has the team started looking at the owned-retail and 47brand.com file as its own segment, or is it still rolled up with everything else?
email2_body: the reason i ask -- the brands we work with in apparel (bombas, vera bradley, talbots) consistently see mail-acquired customers running ~103% higher LTV than digitally-acquired. for a sport-licensed brand with real seasonality drops, a 4-6 drop catalog cadence usually pays back inside year one. worth a 20 min look at your file size and AOV mix?
email3_subject: what postseason MLB does to your file
email3_body: every october your ecom file probably spikes hard, then most of those buyers go quiet until the next run. that's the exact pattern direct mail solves -- frequency testing against the dormant segment, productivity lift of 20-30% in year one for brands we've onboarded. open to seeing the model on '47's numbers?
email4_subject: re: fenway team store + the rest of the file
email4_body: won't keep chasing, kelsey. if direct mail is on the roadmap for 2026 -- even just as a test against your owned-retail and ecom file -- happy to send over how we structured the first year for serena & lily and anthropologie. otherwise i'll close this out. either's fine.

title: Fractional CMO
dossier_summary: Modern luxury furniture, DTC + showrooms, already running US catalog (in DM channel). Categories: beds, sofas, tables, storage, outdoor. Premium AOV. Alexa is fractional across multiple brands (Modloft, Jennifer Zeuner Jewelry). Heavy ecomm/digital background. Time-poor — get to point fast.
email1_body: alexa, saw modloft is already mailing a catalog in the US, which puts you ahead of most modern furniture brands still treating DM as optional. we ran the DM program at design within reach for years and lifted productivity ~20% in year one by rebuilding the segmentation around mail-acquired vs digitally-acquired buyers. those two files behave very differently in the home category. worth comparing notes on how your file is segmented today?
email2_body: quick follow-up. the pattern we see across schoolhouse, mcgee & co, peacock alley: mail-acquired customers carry 103% higher LTV than digitally-acquired in considered home purchases. higher AOV, longer relationship, more repeat. if modloft hasn't isolated those cohorts yet, that's usually where the biggest unlock is. open to a 20 min call?
email3_body: alexa, one more thought. most furniture brands we onboard are under-mailing their best customers and over-mailing the cold file. fixing the frequency mix usually moves the needle before any creative or list change. for reference, serena & lily and crate & barrel both run multi-drop annual cadences against tight segments. happy to share what that looks like.
email4_body: alexa, last note from me. given you're fractional and stretched across brands, i won't keep pinging. if catalog strategy moves up the modloft priority list this quarter, we've spent 25 years on premium home brands and would be glad to take a look.

title: SVP of Marketing & Digital
dossier_summary: Havertys (HVT) - 129 stores across 17 Southern/Midwestern states, founded 1885. Q1 2026: Net sales $189.1M (+4.1%), comp-store +4.3%, written business +6.4%. Design consultants drove 35.3% of written business (up from 33.2%), designer AOV is 2x+ overall. Opened 129th store in St. Louis April 2026. New leases: Dallas, Atlanta, Fredericksburg VA. Helen joined as SVP 2019 from Fitzco/McCann.
email1_body: helen, the q1 number that stood out wasn't the 4.1% top-line, it was design consultants moving from 33.2% to 35.3% of written business with average tickets 2x the house. that's a customer file built for catalog cadence, not just digital retargeting. we run direct mail for serena & lily, design within reach, crate & barrel, schoolhouse, mcgee & co. dwr saw a 20%+ productivity lift year one. worth comparing notes on how a designer-led buyer responds to mail vs digital?
email2_body: helen, follow-up on the designer file question. the part that usually surprises retailers: mail-acquired customers carry 103% higher lifetime value than digitally-acquired on premium home brands we run. for a considered purchase like a sectional or dining set, the gap widens. open to a 20-min call to walk through what frequency testing has looked like for similar store-and-dtc mixes?
email3_body: helen, with st. louis open and dallas, atlanta, fredericksburg leases signed, you've got four trade areas where prospect mail can do the work before the doors open. we typically see 3-8x roas on new-customer drops in markets like these. one iconic home retailer we work with cut circulation 37% while driving 64% more demand. happy to share the segmentation logic.
email4_body: helen, won't keep knocking. if drops per year, segmentation, or the mail vs digital ltv question is on your 2026 roadmap, i'd value the conversation whenever timing fits. if it's not, no offense taken. either way, congrats on the design program momentum.
